result:
I.

The rain comes sideways in the city tonight, the kind of rain that doesn’t fall so much as migrate. It needles across the windows of the elevated train like a thousand silver fish trying to get inside. I’m sitting in the last car with my coat collar up and my headphones leaking something slow and minor-key, watching the reflection of my own tired face superimposed over warehouses and billboards and the occasional burst of neon from a corner bodega that still hasn’t closed for the night.

There is a particular loneliness that only appears after midnight in big cities. It isn’t the loneliness of being alone. It’s the loneliness of being among millions of people who are all pretending they aren’t pretending. Everyone is performing competence, performance, indifference. The woman three seats down keeps checking her phone with the same gesture—wrist flick, thumb swipe, small frown—every forty seconds exactly. I’ve been counting. She’s waiting for something that isn’t coming, or maybe she’s trying to convince herself it already has.

I get off at the wrong stop on purpose. I do this sometimes. The station is unfamiliar, the street signs in a language half memory and half invention. The rain has eased into something gentler, almost polite. I walk without destination, letting my feet negotiate the wet pavement like an old argument they’ve had many times before.

This is how I write, too. Not by sitting down with intention but by wandering into the sentence and seeing what’s already living there. Most days I feel like a medium more than an author—less creator than witness. The stories are out there, walking around in other people’s coats, arguing in other people’s kitchens, losing their keys in other people’s dreams. My only job is to be porous enough to let them through.

II.

Yesterday I watched a man teach his daughter how to tie her shoes on the subway steps. She was maybe five, all knees and serious intention. He didn’t kneel in front of her like in the movies. Instead he sat beside her on the dirty concrete and tied his own shoe very slowly while narrating everything he did. “The rabbit goes around the tree… but he gets lost… so he goes around the other way…” The girl copied him with her smaller hands, tongue poking out in concentration. When she succeeded she didn’t cheer. She simply looked up at him with an expression of such pure, shattering trust that I had to look away. Some moments are too private to witness even by accident.

I’ve been thinking about trust a lot lately. Not the easy kind, the kind we give to people who have already proven themselves. I mean the original trust. The terrifying kind. The kind that small children extend to the world before they learn better. Before they learn that the world is not, in fact, primarily interested in their survival.

We spend the rest of our lives trying to get that original trust back, or trying to prove it was never necessary in the first place. Both projects are more or less doomed. But we keep at them anyway because the alternative is walking around with an open wound in the shape of a question.

III.

I want to tell you about the kindness of strangers, but I don’t want to sentimentalize it. Kindness isn’t always gentle. Sometimes it’s brusque, pragmatic, almost violent in its directness.

Last winter I was walking through Tompkins Square Park at dusk when an old Puerto Rican man in a Yankees jacket started shouting at me. “Hey! Hey you! College boy!” I wasn’t in college. I was thirty-four and looked it. But I stopped anyway. He jabbed a finger at my chest. “Your left boot is untied. You gonna break your goddamn neck.” Then he walked away without waiting for thanks, muttering about idiots who don’t know how to dress themselves.

I tied my boot. The lace had been dragging for six blocks. I never saw him again. But for weeks afterward I felt oddly cared for, as if the city itself had reached out through this rude old man to keep me from falling.

That’s the thing about New York. It will insult you, overcharge you, push you, ignore you for decades. Then one day it will save your life in a way so casual you almost miss it. The transaction is never acknowledged. Both parties pretend it never happened. This is the agreement.

IV.

I’ve been trying to write a novel for three years that refuses to be written. Every time I think I understand what it’s about, it changes its clothes and walks out the door. Currently it believes it is a book about a woman who collects other people’s memories. She has a storage unit in Queens filled with cardboard boxes labeled with dates and locations: “Coney Island, July 1997 – First Kiss,” “Barnes & Noble on 82nd, March 2014 – Panic Attack,” “Rikers visiting room, 2009 – Last conversation with father.”

The problem is the novel doesn’t want to be about memory. It wants to be about the moment right before memory becomes memory. The shimmering edge where experience is still raw and unnamed. The woman keeps trying to collect these moments but they keep dissolving in her hands like cotton candy in rain. I’m starting to suspect the book is actually about failure, but the book itself hasn’t admitted this yet. We’re in a standoff.

Sometimes I wonder if all writing is a form of stalking. You follow an idea around for months or years, learning its habits, where it likes to drink its coffee, what it’s afraid of. You try not to scare it off. You try to become part of its landscape so it forgets you’re there. Then one day, if you’re very lucky and very patient, it turns around and looks at you directly and something passes between you that feels like recognition.

Most of the time it just keeps walking.

V.

There’s a jazz club in the East Village that only exists between 2:17 and 2:43 a.m. on alternating Thursdays. I’ve been there exactly once. The trumpet player had no eyes. Not in the metaphorical sense—he had smooth skin where his eyes should have been. He played like a man trying to remember the taste of colors. The audience was maybe eleven people, including a woman who appeared to be weeping upward; her tears moved from her jaw to her eyes instead of the other way around. I ordered something called “regret on ice” and it tasted like every apology I never gave my mother.

When I left, the bouncer told me I couldn’t come back. “Not yet,” he said. “You still think music is about the notes.” Then he gave me a matchbook with nothing written inside. I still have it.

I keep it in the same drawer as the unsent letters and the photograph of my grandfather holding a fish he caught in 1953. The fish looks more surprised than proud. My grandfather looks like he’s keeping a secret from the camera. I never found out what it was. He died when I was seven, taking most of his silences with him.

VI.

I’ve been thinking about how every city contains every other city. Not metaphorically. Literally. The same way a tree contains every tree that came before it in its rings. Walk down any street in any metropolis at the right hour and you can feel the ghost architecture pressing against the present. Roman forums under London supermarkets. Mayan ball courts beneath Mexico City McDonald’s. The buffalo trails that became Broadway.

Time doesn’t pass so much as accumulate. We’re all walking around inside a palimpsest. Every love that ever happened here is still happening. Every violence. Every act of sudden, inexplicable grace. The city remembers even when we don’t. Especially when we don’t.

This is why certain street corners make you inexplicably sad. Why some alleys feel holy. Why you can stand in the middle of Times Square at rush hour and feel like you’re underwater, drowning in other people’s futures.

VII.

My father used to say that adulthood is mostly realizing that all the grown-ups were making it up as they went along. He said this while drinking whiskey at 3 p.m. on a Tuesday, so I took it with appropriate grains of salt. But he wasn’t wrong. The longer I live, the more I understand that almost nobody knows what they’re doing. The surgeons are improvising. The presidents are guessing. The people who wrote the instruction manuals threw them out years ago and have been winging it ever since.

This should be terrifying. Instead it’s weirdly comforting. There’s a kind of democracy in universal incompetence. We’re all just tall children with credit cards and anxiety disorders, trying to keep the plants alive and remember to call our mothers.

I called my mother yesterday. We talked about the weather in two different states and whether I was eating enough vegetables. She asked if I was happy and I said “mostly” which seemed to satisfy her. I didn’t tell her that happiness feels increasingly like a skill I’m still learning. Like woodworking or playing the cello. Some days I can manage a simple melody. Other days I just produce noise and splinters.

VIII.

There’s a bird that lives in the courtyard behind my building. I don’t know what kind. It’s small and brownish and has opinions. Every morning at 4:47 it begins screaming at the concept of morning. Not singing. Screaming. The sound is pure distilled annoyance at the fact that the sun has once again had the audacity to rise. I respect this bird. Its commitment to its bitterness is admirable. Most of us dilute our rage with coffee and small talk. This bird lets it rip, full volume, no chaser.

I’ve started leaving it seeds. Not because I want it to like me. I just want to be part of whatever ritual it’s performing. Maybe in a thousand years ornithologists will write papers about the mysterious brown bird of the Lower East Side that received ritual offerings of sunflower seeds from an unknown primate. Maybe they’ll speculate about our religious practices. I hope they get it wrong in interesting ways.

IX.

I keep having variations of the same dream. I’m in a house I’ve never seen before but know intimately. Every room contains a different version of myself at different ages. The eight-year-old me is building something complicated with Legos in the kitchen. The twenty-three-year-old me is crying in the bathtub with all his clothes on. The version of me that doesn’t exist yet—the one I might still become—is standing at the top of the stairs saying nothing, just watching.

They never acknowledge each other. They exist in parallel, like radio stations bleeding into each other. I move through the house trying to clean up after all of them. I pick up the Legos. I drain the bathtub. I try to ask the future me what happens next but he only shakes his head like I’ve asked an impossible question.

I always wake up before I reach the attic.

X.

The thing I keep coming back to, the thing I don’t have language for yet, is how beautiful it all is despite everything. Not because of everything. Despite. The distinction matters.

The world is ending in the usual ways and beginning in unusual ones. Someone is playing saxophone on a fire escape three blocks away right now and it sounds like forgiveness. Someone else is probably being born. Someone is choosing to stay alive for one more day even though every part of them wants to stop. These things are all happening at once, layered on top of each other like the city itself. Like music. Like memory.

I don’t know what the point is. I don’t think there is a point in the way we want there to be. The point might be the accumulating. The way every small act of attention adds to the ring count of the tree. The way the bird’s 4:47 a.m. rage somehow makes the silence afterward more complete. The way my unwritten novel and I are teaching each other how to fail better.

I’m 2500 words in and I still haven’t said what I meant to say. Maybe that’s what I meant to say.

The rain has stopped. The city is doing that thing it does where it holds its breath right before dawn, like it’s deciding whether today will be the day it finally tells us all its secrets. It never does. But it keeps offering the possibility, which might be the same thing.

I think I’ll go for a walk.

The streets will be empty except for the people who are always there—the delivery guys, the nurses getting off night shift, the man who talks to pigeons like they owe him money. We’ll nod at each other in recognition. Not because we know each other. Because we’re all out here collecting moments that won’t fit in any storage unit. Because we’re all failing upward in our various ridiculous ways. Because the bird is screaming and the city is listening and somewhere a woman is tying her daughter’s shoes while explaining rabbits and trees.

And for one brief, impossible second, it all feels like it might be enough.

(Word count: 2498)

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
